data.title: PREVIEW: Adobe shares up 1%; results due after the closing bell
data.description:
** Shares of Adobe are up 1.1%, with the Photoshop maker expected to report higher Q1 rev and EPS when it reports results after Wed's closing bell

** Broader mkt was also higher, with Nasdaq last up 1.4%

** Analysts expect Adobe qtrly rev to rise to $5.66 bln vs $5.18 bln in the yr-ago qtr, while they see it posting adj EPS of $4.97/shr vs $4.48 a yr ago, according to LSEG

** On Dec. 11, Adobe forecast fiscal 2025 rev below Wall Street estimates, suggesting to some that the company's investments to weave AI into its software were taking longer than expected to give results

** Jefferies analysts, who have a "buy" rating on ADBE, write in a recent note: "Could F1Q25 act as a positive catalyst? We believe the combination of bearish sentiment and undemanding est. creates an attractive setup."

** Of the 40 analysts who cover ADBE, 28 rate it a "strong buy" or "buy," 11 rate it a "hold" and one rates it a "sell," per LSEG

** ADBE median PT is $600.00; stock last at $438.35

** ADBE down 1.1% YTD, while Nasdaq down 8.5% in that period

data.title: D-Wave rises as research paper shows its quantum computer outperformed classical supercomputer
data.description:
** Shares of quantum computing firm D-Wave rise 8% to $6

** Co said a research paper published in the journal Science showed that its quantum computer outperformed one of the world's most powerful classical supercomputers in solving real-world problems

** "This research proves that D-Wave's quantum computers can reliably solve quantum dynamics problems that could lead to discovery of new materials," said Andrew King, senior scientist at D-Wave

** D-Wave's shares rose 192.4% in the past 12 months

data.title: LVMH Names Bernard Arnault's Son as Loro Piana CEO
data.description:
(17:02 GMT) *LVMH Names Damien Bertrand as Deputy CEO of Louis Vuitton
(17:02 GMT) *LVMH: Frederic Arnault Is Appointed CEO of Loro Piana
(17:03 GMT) *LVMH: Pierre-Emmanuel Angeloglou Is Appointed Deputy CEO of Christian Dior Couture
(17:03 GMT) *LVMH: Frederic Arnault Is Currently CEO of LVMH Watches
(17:04 GMT) *LVMH: Replacement as CEO of LVMH Watches Will Be Announced Shortly
(17:05 GMT) *LVMH: New CEO of Fendi Will Be Announced Shortly
(17:06 GMT) *LVMH: Angeloglou Led Brand Portfolio Within Fashion Group Division, Oversaw Directly Fendi
(17:24 GMT) LVMH Names Bernard Arnault's Son as Loro Piana CEO

By Mauro Orru

Bernard Arnault's luxury-goods conglomerate LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton appointed Frederic Arnault, son of the French billionaire, to the helm of Italian fashion house Loro Piana.

The company said Arnault would join Loro Piana on March 26 for a transition period with current Chief Executive Damien Bertrand before taking over on June 10. Arnault, who is currently CEO of LVMH Watches, will report to Toni Belloni, chairman of LVMH Italy.

LVMH appointed Bertrand as deputy CEO of the Louis Vuitton brand as of June 10. Bertrand, who will report to Louis Vuitton CEO Pietro Beccari, will become a member of the LVMH executive committee in January next year.

Meanwhile, Pierre-Emmanuel Angeloglou will take over as deputy CEO of Christian Dior Couture as of April 15.

Write to Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

data.title: UK Shares Edge Up after 6-Day Loss
data.description:
The FTSE 100 rose 0.3%, snapping a six-day losing streak, as investors welcomed a softer-than-expected US inflation report despite escalating trade tensions.

The latest US Consumer Price Index (CPI) showed a 2.8% annual increase in February, down from January's 3% and below forecasts of 2.9%, easing concerns about aggressive Federal Reserve action.

In the UK, Prime Minister Keir Starmer expressed hope that Britain could avoid US tariffs on steel and aluminum, stating the government would pursue a "pragmatic approach" while keeping all options open.

Among individual stocks, Rolls-Royce gained over 3.5%, while banking stocks rallied, with Barclays up more than 3%, NatWest around 3%, Standard Chartered nearly 3%, and HSBC Holdings 0.7%.

Pharma giant AstraZeneca advanced over 1%, while oil majors Shell and BP rose 0.6% and 0.9%, respectively.

Gold miner Fresnillo jumped more than 4% as investors sought safety amid ongoing global trade uncertainties.

data.title: BBVA eyes doubling investment banking revenues, report says
data.description:
Spain's BBVA plans to double revenues in its corporate and investment banking unit in the next few years as part of a new strategy to be announced later in 2025, Bloomberg News reported on Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The unit in question finished last year with a record revenue of 5.8 billion euros ($6.33 billion), a rise of 22% compared to 2023. Total revenue at Spain's second-largest lender by market value reached almost 35.5 billion euros in 2024.

Bloomberg reported that BBVA, which has announced a 12-billion-euro hostile takeover bid for Sabadell , is likely to say it wants to achieve the new revenue goal at its investment banking business by 2029.

The growth push will be underpinned by fresh hiring and include a plan to substantially boost the size of BBVA's Brazil office, unidentified sources cited by Bloomberg said.

A spokesperson for BBVA declined to comment.

($1 = 0.9168 euros)

data.title: UK care firms should hire foreign workers already in country, says government
data.description:
British social care providers will have to prioritise hiring foreign care workers already in the country before recruiting from overseas, the government said on Wednesday, as it cracks down on worker abuse in a sector heavily reliant on immigration.

The move follows the introduction in November of stronger sanctions for rogue employers who recruit foreign workers but failed to provide them with work, leaving some unemployed and close to destitution.

Others are out of work due to the government revoking the licenses of rogue employers to hire overseas workers.

A survey of more than 3,000 people on health and care worker visas last month showed many had paid fraudulent fees to secure a job only to find no work after arriving in Britain. It also highlighted that some were living in overcrowded housing  and earning less than the minimum wage.

Care providers in England who wish to recruit from overseas will now have to show they have first tried to hire someone with a care sector visa who is already in the country but out of work, Britain's Home Office said.

"Those who have come to the UK to support our adult care sector should have the opportunity to do so, free from abuse and exploitation," Migration Minister Seema Malhotra said.

Nearly a third of all care workers in England are migrants, many having arrived from countries such as Nigeria, Zimbabwe, India and the Philippines to fill thousands of vacancies after Britain left the European Union and to meet the healthcare demands of an ageing population.

Charities and trade unions say Britain's post-Brexit system of allowing companies to sponsor workers to receive a visa empowers unscrupulous employers who can use the threat of deportation to abuse workers.

data.title: Cintas Checks Show In-Line Third-Quarter Revenue, RBC Says
data.description:
Cintas is tracking toward in-line revenue for its fiscal third quarter as some of the headwinds from the previous quarter abate, RBC Capital Markets said in a note e-mailed Wednesday.

The brokerage expects third-quarter revenue of about $2.6 billion, which it said would match Wall Street's views. RBC expects earnings of $1.06 a share, compared with the Street's $1.05 estimate, according to a note to clients. The company is scheduled to report results March 26.

The uniform supplier's organic revenue growth is likely to accelerate to 7.4% year over year from 7.1% in the second quarter, reflecting gains across uniform rental, first aid and fire protection. RBC sees uniform direct sales dropping 3% in the third quarter, according to the note.

Second-quarter revenue was impacted by weaker catalog and direct sales, though Cintas indicated that catalog was functioning at a high level and the macro backdrop was stable, RBC analysts Ashish Sabadra and David Paige said.

"Although we expect those headwinds to abate, we monitor for any slowdown in uniform rental revenues resulting from softening employment trends," the analysts said. RBC is monitoring the potential effects of tariffs, but said Cintas' flexible supply chain should mitigate some of the impact over the longer term.

Despite the challenges, the company is expected to deliver above-industry revenue growth and margin expansion amid its "solid execution," digital transformation measures, and focus on recession-resilient verticals, according to RBC.

Cintas is "more likely" to reaffirm its full-year revenue growth outlook due to limited bolt-on mergers and acquisitions in the third quarter, the analysts said. RBC projects annual organic revenue growth of 7.5%, ahead of the Street's views for a 7.3% gain.

"We are more inclined to see an increase to the EPS guide of ($4.28 to $4.34) due to solid operating leverage and opportunistic buybacks, which is not contemplated in the guide," Sabadra and Paige said.

In January, Cintas went public with its attempt in November to acquire smaller rival UniFirst in a potential all-cash offer worth roughly $5.3 billion, which was rejected by UniFirst.

data.title: Why Stitch Fix's turnaround seems to be working, despite the tough economy
data.description:
By Ciara Linnane

Online clothing and styling company's stock soars 19% after it says strong January persisted through March

Stitch Fix Inc.'s stock soared 19% early Wednesday, reflecting late Tuesday gains, as investors cheered the online clothing and styling service's upbeat sales guidance and positive comments on performance in the first three months of the year.

Mizuho analysts highlighted remarks from Chief Executive Matt Baer on the earnings call that strength in January had continued through February and March, positive news after the company's fiscal second-quarter earnings beat expectations.

"With market concerns of consumer spending slowing and recession fears now building, we did not have an improving SFIX model on our bingo card," analysts led by David Bellinger wrote in a note to clients published early Wednesday.

Still, the research team noted challenges as Stitch Fix's (SFIX) management continues to push through a turnaround plan in a "highly dynamic demand environment."

Mizuho is sticking with its underperform rating on the stock and kept its price target at $3, below its close Tuesday at $4.23.

Baer told analysts that the Stitch Fix customer spans all income levels as most U.S. consumers are unhappy with the in-store shopping experience for clothing right now. The executive cited market data suggesting just 10% of consumers are satisfied when they go to stores for clothes.

"About 90% of people feel stress when deciding either what to buy or what to wear when they're getting dressed each day," he said, according to a FactSet transcript.

"And that stress and anxiety really coupled with the challenges that they have with the alternative shopping methods is why the core value proposition of Stitch Fix is one that resonates so strongly."

In response to questions on the impact of President Donald Trump's tariffs, Baer said Stich Fix has created a task force to create a mitigation strategy. However, the company expects its "vast matrix" of national and market brands to enable it to quickly shift within its portfolio.

"We're not currently anticipating any impact to our margin or any required increase in costs clients are facing," he said.

The company posted a net loss of 5 cents a share for the quarter, narrower than the year-earlier number and better than the 11-cent loss anticipated by FactSet analysts.

Sales fell 5.5% to $312.1 million but exceeded the FactSet consensus of $298.1 million.

Active clients slid 15.5% year over year to around 2.37 million. Wall Street expected around 2.36 million.

"Our clients are responding to the improvements we've made to our experience, including the increased newness in our assortment, expanded Fix flexibility, and investments in stronger client-stylist relationships," Baer said in prepared remarks.

Stitch Fix has spent months trying to deepen the relationships between shoppers and the company's stylists who help them pick out clothing.

The company said it expects total fiscal-year sales of $1.225 billion to $1.240 billion, representing a year-over-year drop.

However, that forecast is up from expectations given in December for $1.14 billion to $1.18 billion. Tuesday's forecast was above FactSet analyst estimates of $1.18 billion. The company's fiscal 2025 runs through July.

"Recent sequential improvements are largely a function of a depressed, underlying core business with some fresh initiatives showing early results, in our view," the Mizuho analysts wrote.

Stitch Fix's stock has gained 81% in the last 12 months, while the S&P 500 SPX has gained 7.7%.

-Ciara Linnane

data.title: Subtle Signs of Tariffs Pass-Through Detected in CPI Report — Market Talk
data.description: February's CPI brings "subtle notes of tariff pass-through," BNP Paribas' Andy Schneider and Britney Jackson write, looking into categories where imports are relevant. Core goods excluding used vehicles rose 0.12% in the month, compared to a flat prior three-month average and its strongest monthly pace since September. "Apparel (particularly jewelry and watches), personal care products and miscellaneous personal goods prices were firm," they say, adding that there was "minor movement in other goods categories that we flagged as sensitive to Chinese imports such as household furnishings, consumer electronics and toys." Schneider and Jackson expect greater upward movement in these categories this month. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1318 ET - February's CPI brings "subtle notes of tariff pass-through," BNP Paribas' Andy Schneider and Britney Jackson write, looking into categories where imports are relevant. Core goods excluding used vehicles rose 0.12% in the month, compared to a flat prior three-month average and its strongest monthly pace since September. "Apparel (particularly jewelry and watches), personal care products and miscellaneous personal goods prices were firm," they say, adding that there was "minor movement in other goods categories that we flagged as sensitive to Chinese imports such as household furnishings, consumer electronics and toys." Schneider and Jackson expect greater upward movement in these categories this month. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1303 ET - Alcoa has two smelters in the U.S. that produce about 290,000 metric tons of aluminum annually, both of which stand to benefit from tariffs enacted on imports of steel and aluminum, CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "However, our Canadian operations produce three times that amount," she says. A 25% tariff on the aluminum that Alcoa produces in Canada would be a net negative for the company, resulting in the loss of tens of millions of dollars, Beerman says. The loss isn't likely to hurt the company's first quarter, but could affect future earnings, she adds. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1258 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper up 1% at $9,771.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.1% at $2,701.50 a ton. Markets have been rocked by President Trump's tariffs, Trade Nation's David Morrison says in a note. After Canadian, Mexican and Chinese levies, Wednesday saw the imposition of additional 25% tariffs on U.S. imports of aluminum and steel. The price of U.S. aluminum over LME prices has surged in response as traders scramble to import the metal. There have been tariff postponements, exceptions and retaliation and investors are struggling to keep up, Morrison writes. But the overall sense is that tariffs are no longer just a threat that precedes negotiations, Morrison says. Under the current administration, they are a weapon--however blunt--that Trump is prepared to employ, he says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1254 ET - Alcoa is working closely with President Trump's administration, as well as trade groups in the U.S. and Canada, to decipher the effect that tariffs on steel and aluminum will have on the industry, CFO Molly Beerman says at the J.P. Morgan Industrials Conference. "While we're very supportive of (the administration's) efforts to improve the industry, as well as strengthen U.S. manufacturing jobs, we do see that there could be some harm from the tariffs," she says, adding that Alcoa is particularly focused on gaining a Canadian exemption. The company is also having discussions about energy policy, because "affordable and access to energy, particularly renewable, is critical in any decision for additional aluminum investment in the long term," Beerman says. Shares rise 3.3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1251 ET - Tariffs on steel and aluminum are already creating significant uncertainty in the market, Alcoa CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "Some customers are rushing to secure supply ahead of the tariffs, while others are playing wait and see, to see how the final tariff structure will exist," she says. "As a result this quarter, we do have more uncertainty in both our revenue and our working capital that is more than normal." Overall, though, Alcoa is expecting what Beerman calls a strong first quarter, boosted by higher costs for the metal. Shares rise 3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1216 ET - Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem says officials are determined to not let inflation accelerate beyond the 2% target during this time of trade tension. Most of the central bank's communications, alongside its decision to make a quarter-point rate cut, is focused on the risk of higher prices that tariffs bring. He says some prices will go up due to tariffs, in contrast to arguments from Trump administration officials. "What we don't want to see," Macklem says at a press conference, "is that first round of price increases have knock-on effects, causing other prices to go up. That becomes generalized and ongoing inflation. That's what we can't let happen here." (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1148 ET - President Trump's new tariffs on aluminum and steel will take a toll on the entire auto industry, with some of the biggest price surges expected for larger-model vehicles like pickup trucks and SUVs, Kevin Roberts of CarGurus says in a note. The scale of the price hikes will depend on each automaker's sourcing and production chain, he says, but vehicles that use more materials will see the biggest markups, Roberts says. The levies will have a more immediate impact on the prices of used cars if the cost of auto parts goes up, he says. The used market is more supply-restrained, especially given high demand for vehicles that are three years old or younger, so anyone considering those models should purchase sooner rather than later, Roberts says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1103 ET - Live cattle futures on the CME are up 1.1%, pushing the most-active futures contract over the $2.01 a pound mark. For cattle, supportive fundamentals are striking a balance with an increasingly stormier macroeconomic view. "Traders being pushed from two directions," says ADM Investor Services in a note. "One side has boxed beef prices rapidly rising but at the same time traders are alarmed there is talk about a recession and financial markets falling." New retaliatory tariffs from the EU include beef and pork products. Lean hogs are up 0.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0939 ET - A slower-than-expected pace of price increases in February gives the Fed some breathing room, but tariffs may still spoil the party, Global X's Scott Helfstein writes. CPI slowed to 2.8% from 3%. "This CPI number is encouraging but does not factor in the new round of tariffs," Helfstein says. "That might begin showing up next month, but the lag could be longer." Helfstein expects volatility to remain elevated as markets await the impact. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0937 ET - The European Union announced its own retaliatory tariffs against the U.S., with meats, dairy, and crops like soybeans and wheat are among the targets of the EU's countermeasures. "The latest Trump tariff consequence (at least temporarily) wiped that out steady-to-higher trend quickly," says Matt Zeller of StoneX in a note. Most-active corn is down 1.9% , soybeans fall 0.9%, and wheat slides 0.4%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0823 ET - President Trump's recent swing at Canada took aim at dairy tariffs, but Scotiabank economist Derek Holt is dismissive of the complaint. Holt says U.S. dairy producers don't pay tariffs when exporting into Canada, since the tariff rate on U.S. products kick in at very high levels and U.S. exports are a fraction of the set caps. He adds the U.S. uses the same protective system which was included in the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement negotiated during Trump's first term in office. "And if we want to talk about distorting agricultural sector policies...start by ending the hundreds of billions of dollars in subsidies your [U.S.] government grants corporate farms each year through the wasteful Farm Bill." (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0816 ET - The dollar could recover meaningfully if the U.S. avoids a recession as some market participants fear, Commerzbank currency analyst Volkmar Baur says in a note. Uncertainty over U.S. trade policy is elevated, with President Trump's back and forth on tariffs weighing on sentiment, he says. The market is betting that the risk of recession will increase significantly with each additional round of tit-for-tat tariffs, he says. That is why stocks, Treasury yields and the dollar are falling, he says. However, hard U.S. economic data so far look "pretty decent" with the exception of weak retail sales in January, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 103.622. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1318 ET - February's CPI brings "subtle notes of tariff pass-through," BNP Paribas' Andy Schneider and Britney Jackson write, looking into categories where imports are relevant. Core goods excluding used vehicles rose 0.12% in the month, compared to a flat prior three-month average and its strongest monthly pace since September. "Apparel (particularly jewelry and watches), personal care products and miscellaneous personal goods prices were firm," they say, adding that there was "minor movement in other goods categories that we flagged as sensitive to Chinese imports such as household furnishings, consumer electronics and toys." Schneider and Jackson expect greater upward movement in these categories this month. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1301 ET - Inflation data aren't giving the Fed the signals for cutting interest rates, TS Lombard's Steven Blitz writes. Although February CPI slowed to 2.8% from 3%, "there are enough anomalies in the data to make suspect any attempt to say this is trend." Blitz says that goods ex-food and energy increased at a 2.7% seasonally adjusted annual rate in the month, an improvement from January's 3.5% but still sticky. This is "the category where the impact of tariffs is going to be most evident in the first round," he says. "In the end, employment continues to rise, and inflation is sticky to rising with it." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1249 ET - Gold futures gain as market turmoil deepens on U.S. tariff uncertainty and economic data. Futures rise 0.8% to $2,944 a troy ounce, nearing the late-February high of $2,974/oz. President Trump's stop-and-start tariffs are compounding market fears over the U.S. economy amid escalating talk of a recession, XS.com's Samer Hasn says in a note. This is pushing investors towards safe-haven assets like gold. At the same time, the release of Consumer Price Index data for February showed U.S. inflation slowed to 2.8%, lower than expected, raising hopes the Federal Reserve will ease monetary policy. Lower interest rates are typically a boon for non-interest bearing bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1244 ET - Besides its strident tone on inflation risks, the Bank of Canada's communications on its latest quarter-point rate cut also dropped any reference to excess slack, or spare capacity, in the economy, say economists at National Bank Financial. Instead, the BOC says the economy was on solid footing entering 2025, given the much stronger-than-expected growth in 4Q and 3Q of last year. NBF reckons that slack remains in the economy. Nevertheless, the BOC's evolving view on inflation risks "suggests the bar to rapid rate relief is somewhat higher than we'd thought," NBF says. The firm adds the BOC will lean heavily on data, and "won't simply cut in anticipation of economic weakness," from a trade conflict.(paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1240 ET - Forward guidance is impossible in this frequently-changing economic backdrop dominated by threats of hefty tariffs from the US on Canadian imports, says Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem. "We're going to get weaker economic activity. We're going to get higher prices, higher inflation. We can't change that," Macklem says at a press conference. "What we can do is ensure that any rise in inflation is temporary." BOC did some special polling ahead of Wednesday's decision, and the data show short-term inflation expectations are rising. "It's going to be critically important that medium and longer term inflation expectations remain well anchored," Macklem says. (paul.vieira@wsj.com;@paulvieira)

1233 ET - Economists say Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem's remarks regarding inflation risks from the U.S.-Canada trade conflict can be considered "hawkish." Traders appear to agree, with Canadian dollar strengthening slightly versus USD, and Canada bond yields across the curve moving upward. "Monetary policy cannot offset the impacts of a trade war," Macklem says. "What it can and must do is ensure that higher prices do not lead to ongoing inflation." BOC is likely counting on governments at the federal and provincial level to make cash available to help affected workers and businesses. Canada has allocated C$6.5 billion in available financing for trade-exposed firms, to either deal with negative ramifications or find new markets for their wares. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1216 ET - Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem says officials are determined to not let inflation accelerate beyond the 2% target during this time of trade tension. Most of the central bank's communications, alongside its decision to make a quarter-point rate cut, is focused on the risk of higher prices that tariffs bring. He says some prices will go up due to tariffs, in contrast to arguments from Trump administration officials. "What we don't want to see," Macklem says at a press conference, "is that first round of price increases have knock-on effects, causing other prices to go up. That becomes generalized and ongoing inflation. That's what we can't let happen here." (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1216 ET - Germany's draft budget plan, to be voted on in the next week, should revive German growth, which has been absent for years, BNP Paribas economists say in a note. Proposals from the likely incoming governing coalition partners could generate almost 120 billion euros in additional annual expenditure, including 50 billion euros for infrastructure and 70 billion euros for defense should Germany raise defense spending to 3.5% of GDP, they say. "This spending would directly stimulate public consumption and investment and indirectly stimulate the economy as a whole." German growth could therefore reach 0.9% in 2025 and 2026, higher than BNP's current forecasts of 0.2% and 0.5% respectively, and 2 percentage points higher than currently expected in 2029, they say. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1211 ET - Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem says senior officials did not seriously contemplate a half-point rate cut, noting that some so-called hard data, like GDP, was encouraging. "You can't see much damage from the trade war [in] the hard data yet," he says at a press conference in Ottawa, after delivering a quarter-point cut to 2.75%. Macklem says rate policy needs to be forward looking. While survey results point to "quite weak" domestic demand in 1Q, the U.S.-Canada trade conflict threatens to increase prices and inflation, Macklem says. "We've got to be very careful to balance those two," says the governor, whose mandate is to set rate policy to achieve and maintain 2% inflation. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1202 ET - At-home food prices are still high but inflation is stabilizing after four straight months of acceleration, D.A. Davidson analyst Michael Baker says. The 1.8% inflation rate for that category, down from 1.9% the month before, could translate to higher comp sales at stores like BJ's, Costco, Target and Walmart. But chronically high food prices could erode margins and threaten customers' ability to add more discretionary products to their carts, Baker says. Meanwhile, pet food inflation increased, ending six straight months of declines as pricing in that category has become a negative for related sales. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1153 ET - TwentyFour asset management prefers U.K. corporate bonds over their government bond equivalents due to concerns about the U.K.'s rising public financing needs. At the upcoming March 26 budget, U.K. Treasury chief Rachel Reeves is likely to announce spending cuts within the welfare system to create room for higher defense spending, Felipe Villarroel partner at TwentyFour AM says in a note. "With a mind on the generally negative reaction to Reeves' October Budget, we would prefer to remain on the sidelines when it comes to gilts," Villarroel says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1148 ET - President Trump's new tariffs on aluminum and steel will take a toll on the entire auto industry, with some of the biggest price surges expected for larger-model vehicles like pickup trucks and SUVs, Kevin Roberts of CarGurus says in a note. The scale of the price hikes will depend on each automaker's sourcing and production chain, he says, but vehicles that use more materials will see the biggest markups, Roberts says. The levies will have a more immediate impact on the prices of used cars if the cost of auto parts goes up, he says. The used market is more supply-restrained, especially given high demand for vehicles that are three years old or younger, so anyone considering those models should purchase sooner rather than later, Roberts says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: HPQ Silicon Confirms "No Impact" From Tariffs on Discussions With Parties
data.description:
HPQ Silicon on Wednesday said the recently announced tariffs and resulting counter tariffs on critical feedstock including fumed silica will not impact discussions with parties that are interested in its fumed-silica material and technologies.

The company also intends to start commercial production of fumed silica using its pilot plant this year.

The development of its Fumed Silica Reactor (FSR) system is progressing as planned. After producing the first batch last month, the pilot plant is now optimizing operations to increase throughput from 20 to 30 kilograms per batch to over 480 kg per day. During this phase, the fumed silica produced will undergo internal testing, with samples sent to parties who has signed letters of intent and other non-disclosure agreements.

By the fourth quarter of this year, HPQ will begin commercial production of fumed silica using its pilot-scale FSR system, making it Canada's first domestic producer of fumed silica.

"Fumed silica is used in thousands of everyday products, yet Canada has no domestic production, leaving Canadian manufacturers vulnerable to unpredictable trade policies that could swiftly drive prices up," said chief executive Bernard Tourillon. "HPQ's Fumed Silica Reactor technology has the potential to change that. Our goal is to use the pilot plant to begin commercial fumed silica production in Canada in Q4 2025. At the same time, we will lay the groundwork to scale up production and establish, over the next few years, a reliable, nationally beneficial supply, thereby reducing reliance on U.S. and other fumed silica imports."

The company's shares were last seen up $0.01 to $0.21 on the TSX Venture Exchange.

data.title: MEDIA ADVISORY: Health Coalition to announce response to court rulings on Bill 7 Charter Challenge and Orchard Villa case
data.description:
TORONTO, March 12, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — The courts have ruled on two cases impacting thousands of elderly hospital patients and long-term care residents in Ontario and the Ontario Health Coalition is holding a press conference to announce their response. The first case, known as the Bill 7 Charter Challenge, concerns the requirement that hospitals charge elderly patients $400 per day while they remain in hospital if they refuse to go to a long-term care home that is not of their choosing. The second case — the Orchard Villa case — concerns the Ford government’s granting of a massive new expansion and 30-year license to a for-profit corporation Southbridge at Orchard Villa where they have a terrible record of inadequate care.

When: Thursday, March 13 at 10 a.m.

Where: Goldblatt Partners Law Firm, 20 Dundas Street West, 10th floor boardroom in person and by Zoom for media across the province. For the Zoom, please click to register here and Zoom will email you the press conference link.

What:

The Ontario Health Coalition and the Advocacy Centre for the Elderly brought a legal challenge to what was known as Bill 7, euphemistically titled the More Beds, Better Care Act (2022), that requires hospitals to charge elderly patients who are classified by the hospital as “Alternate Level of Care” to be charged $400 per day if they refuse to go to a long-term care home not of their choosing. Patients can be sent up to 70 km away in Southern Ontario, and up to 150 km or even further if there are no beds available in Northern Ontario. The court case is a Charter Challenge, known as a Constitutional Challenge or a Challenge under the Charter of Rights. The case was argued before the Ontario Superior Court on Monday, September 23 and Tuesday, September 24. A backgrounder and links to the court filings, evidence and court ruling are available here.

The Ontario Health Coalition and Cathy Parkes, the daughter of a deceased resident, also brought a court challenge seeking a judicial review of the Ford government’s decision to grant an 88-bed expansion and new 30-year license for the 233-bed long-term care home owned by Southbridge at Orchard Villa in Pickering. The Coalition and the families asked the court to quash the license and require the Ford government live up to their own long-term care legislation. Under the Fixing Long-Term Care Act (2021), the government cannot issue licenses to long-term care home owners when their past conduct offers reasonable grounds to believe that the home will be operated in a manner that is prejudicial to the health, safety and welfare of its residents. The case was argued before a panel of judges at the Ontario Divisional Court on Thursday, October 17. A backgrounder and links to the court filings, evidence and court ruling are here.

Who: Steven Shrybman, lawyer, Goldblatt Partners LLB and Natalie Mehra, executive director, Ontario Health Coalition.

For more information: Natalie Mehra, executive director, Ontario Health Coalition cell (416) 230-6402; Salah Shadir, administration & operations director, Ontario Health Coalition cell (647) 648-5706.

data.title: Smithfield Foods stock recovers; CEO points to tariff troubles
data.description:
** Smithfield Foods CEO Shane Smith said Wed that tariffs make it more difficult for the largest U.S. pork processor to sell all parts of a pig; stock recovering 0.9% to $19.44 after recent declines

** SFD now up over 4% in past two sessions, rebounding from a steep selloff since late February

** SFD sold shares at $20 in a weakly received initial public offer on January 7

** CEO Smith highlighted tariffs imposed by China on $21 bln of U.S. agricultural products, affecting offal exports. Those tariffs were a response to tariffs imposed on China by U.S. President Donald Trump

** Smithfield does not export significant meat to China but relies on offal exports such as pig stomachs, hearts and heads, which U.S. consumer generally do not eat. Those exports face increased tariffs

** Canada last week suspended imports from Smithfield's Tar Heel facility due to offal issue, unrelated to tariffs

Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.
